walkthrough.title: Unfold the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Stone Age. Place the game board in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. The board shows all the worker placement locations: the Hunting Grounds, Forest, Clay Pit, Quarry, River, Farm, Tool Maker, Mating Hut, four Civilization Card slots, and Building tile stacks. Take a moment to identify each area so you know where workers will go.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Resource Supply
walkthrough.text: Gather all the resource tokens and organize them near the board. You will need wood, clay, stone, gold, and food tokens. Place them in separate piles where everyone can reach them. These resources are the currency of the game. You will spend them to buy buildings and civilization cards.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Civilization Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the thirty-six civilization cards. Shuffle them into a face-down deck and place it near the board. Deal four cards face up into the four civilization card slots on the board. These cards provide immediate bonuses when purchased and end-game scoring multipliers. They will be a major source of points.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Building Tile Stacks
walkthrough.text: Create building tile stacks equal to the number of players. Each stack should have seven tiles. Place them near the board and flip the top tile of each stack face up so players can see what is available to build. Buildings are purchased with resources and score victory points immediately.

walkthrough.title: Place Tool Tokens
walkthrough.text: Set the tool tokens near the board in a supply. Each player has three tool slots on their player board, and tools start at zero. You will gain tools by sending a worker to the Tool Maker location. Tools are added to your dice rolls when gathering resources, making your workers more productive over time.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Dice
walkthrough.text: Find all seven dice and place them in a shared area. These dice are used by all players when resolving resource gathering. The number of dice you roll depends on how many workers you placed at a resource location. You will be rolling these frequently, so keep them handy.

walkthrough.title: Player Count Adjustments
walkthrough.text: If you are playing with two players, use only two building stacks and note that certain placement locations have reduced worker capacity. Three players use three building stacks. Four players use all four stacks with full capacity at every location. Make sure you have the right number of stacks before dealing starting items.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Give each player a player board. This board tracks three important things: your farm level, which starts at zero, your tool slots, which also start empty, and your resource storage area. Place a score marker for each player on the zero space of the victory point track on the main board.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Starting Workers
walkthrough.text: Each player receives five worker meeples in their color. These are your starting workers. You can expand to a maximum of ten workers by visiting the Mating Hut throughout the game. More workers means more actions each round, so growing your workforce is a priority.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Food
walkthrough.text: Each player takes twelve food tokens from the supply. Food is consumed every round to feed your workers. You must pay one food per worker at the end of each round. With five workers, twelve food gives you a couple of rounds before you need to hunt for more or build up your farm.

walkthrough.title: Confirm Starting State
walkthrough.text: Do a quick check. Each player should have five workers, twelve food, no resources, no tools, and a farm level of zero. The board should have four face-up civilization cards and the correct number of building stacks with top tiles revealed. All board locations should be empty and available. Choose a first player randomly and play proceeds clockwise. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Each round of Stone Age has three phases. First, all players take turns placing workers on the board. Second, all players resolve their placed workers to collect resources and perform actions. Third, everyone must feed their workers. These three phases repeat every round until the game ends. Let's walk through each phase.

walkthrough.title: Phase One: Placing Workers
walkthrough.text: Then the next player places, and so on. You keep going around the table until every player has placed all their workers. The key rule is that on each placement turn, all your workers go to one location.

walkthrough.title: Placement Restrictions
walkthrough.text: Different locations have different capacity limits. The Hunting Grounds can hold unlimited workers from any player. Resource locations like the Forest, Clay Pit, Quarry, and River can hold a maximum of seven workers total across all players. The Farm, Tool Maker, and Mating Hut have special restrictions. The Farm takes exactly one worker from one player per round. The Tool Maker also takes one worker from one player. The Mating Hut takes exactly two workers from one player.

walkthrough.title: Civilization Cards and Buildings
walkthrough.text: Each of the four civilization card slots takes one worker from one player. Each building stack also takes one worker from one player. These are valuable placements because they let you spend resources for victory points and powerful ongoing effects. Workers placed here are resolved during Phase Two.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two: Resolving Workers
walkthrough.text: Starting with the first player, each player resolves all their placed workers one location at a time in any order they choose. When resolving resource locations, you roll dice. When resolving the Farm, Tool Maker, or Mating Hut, you gain the specified permanent upgrade. When resolving civilization cards or buildings, you pay resources to claim them.

walkthrough.title: Resource Gathering with Dice
walkthrough.text: At resource locations, roll one die per worker you placed there. Add up the total. Then divide by the resource's cost: food costs two, wood costs three, clay costs four, stone costs five, and gold costs six. Round down. The result is how many resources you collect. For example, three workers at the Quarry rolling a total of seventeen, divided by five, gives you three stone.

walkthrough.title: Using Tools
walkthrough.text: After rolling dice for resources, you can add your tool values to the total before dividing. Each tool can only be used once per round. Flip the tool to its used side after applying it. Tools reset at the start of the next round. Tools are powerful because they reduce the randomness of dice rolls and boost your resource yields consistently.

walkthrough.title: The Farm
walkthrough.text: Place one worker at the Farm to increase your farm level by one, up to a maximum of ten. Each farm level produces one food automatically during the feeding phase. Building up your farm reduces and eventually eliminates the need to hunt for food, freeing your workers for more productive tasks.

walkthrough.title: The Tool Maker
walkthrough.text: Place one worker at the Tool Maker to gain one tool level. Tools fill three slots on your player board, and each slot can hold values one through four. Your first three tool upgrades fill the three slots at level one. Further upgrades increase existing tools to two, then three, then four. Maximum total tool value is twelve.

walkthrough.title: The Mating Hut
walkthrough.text: Place exactly two workers at the Mating Hut to gain one new worker, added to your supply for next round. You can grow from five workers to a maximum of ten. More workers means more dice at resource locations, more placement options, and more flexibility. Growing your workforce is one of the strongest early investments.

walkthrough.title: Civilization Cards
walkthrough.text: Place one worker on a civilization card slot. To claim the card, pay resources equal to the card's position. The first slot costs one resource matching the symbol on top. The second slot costs two resources, and so on. Cards provide immediate bonuses like free resources, tools, or victory points. More importantly, many cards have end-game multipliers that scale with your tools, farms, buildings, or workers.

walkthrough.title: Buildings
walkthrough.text: Place one worker on a building stack. Pay the specific resource combination shown on the top tile to score its victory points immediately. Some buildings show specific resources required, while others let you choose any combination. After claiming a building, the next tile in the stack is revealed.

walkthrough.title: Phase Three: Feeding
walkthrough.text: After all workers are resolved, everyone must feed their workers. Pay one food per worker you have. Your farm level reduces this cost because each farm level provides one free food. If you cannot feed all your workers, you take a ten-point penalty. You never lose workers to starvation, but that penalty is steep.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Triggers
walkthrough.text: The game ends when either at least one civilization card stack cannot be refilled to four cards, or at least one building tile stack is exhausted. Finish the current round so all players complete placement, resolution, and feeding. Then proceed to final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Add up three categories. First, victory points accumulated during the game from buildings and card bonuses. Second, end-game scoring from civilization card multipliers: tool multipliers, farm multipliers, building multipliers, worker multipliers, and unique set bonuses. Third, each remaining resource and food token is worth one victory point. The player with the most total points wins.

summary.title: Round Structure
bullets: 3 phases per round: Place Workers → Resolve Workers → Feed Workers
Repeat until end-game trigger

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Round
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with four players. Each player will make different placement decisions so you can see how the major locations work. Follow along with the moves even if you have fewer players at your table. Ready? Let's go.

walkthrough.title: Round One Resources
walkthrough.text: Everyone starts with five workers and twelve food. You have no resources, no tools, and your farm is at zero. That means you need to feed five workers at the end of this round, costing five food. You will have seven food left after feeding, so there is no urgency yet. Use this round to invest in permanent upgrades.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Mating Hut
walkthrough.text: This is one of the strongest opening moves. You will gain one new worker, bringing your total to six for next round. More workers means more dice rolls, more placements, and more options every round. The compounding value of an early worker is enormous.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Farm
walkthrough.text: This increases your farm level from zero to one, meaning you will get one free food every round for the rest of the game. The earlier you invest in farming, the more food you save over the entire game. Farming is a long-term investment that pays off handsomely.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Tool Maker
walkthrough.text: You gain your first tool at level one. Tools add to your dice totals when gathering resources. Even a plus-one can turn a bad roll into enough for an extra resource. Tools improve every single resource gathering action for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Player Four: Hunting Grounds
walkthrough.text: You will roll three dice and divide the total by two to get food. With an average roll of ten to eleven, you should get about five food. This is a safe early play, but notice that Players one through three invested in permanent upgrades while you gained a one-time food haul.

walkthrough.title: Remaining Placements
walkthrough.text: Each player has workers remaining. Player one places three workers in the Forest for wood. Player two places three workers at the Clay Pit. Player three places three workers at the Quarry for stone. Player four places two workers at a civilization card slot and the Hunting Grounds. Continue until all workers are placed.

walkthrough.title: Resolving: Player One Rolls for Wood
walkthrough.text: Player one resolves the Forest. Roll three dice. Let's say the total is eleven. Divide by three for wood, giving you three wood. Not bad. Player one also resolves the Mating Hut, gaining a sixth worker for next round. Remember, you choose the order to resolve your locations.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Only Hunting for Food
walkthrough.text: Player four spent all five workers on food and a civilization card. They got plenty of food but no permanent upgrades. Meanwhile, Players one, two, and three have a new worker, a farm level, and a tool respectively. Over the next several rounds, those permanent improvements will generate far more value than any one-time food haul.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Invest in Upgrades
walkthrough.text: Player four, next round you should send workers to the Mating Hut and the Farm or Tool Maker before gathering resources. It feels scary to skip food hunting, but your twelve starting food and remaining supply will sustain you. The permanent upgrades are what separate winners from losers in Stone Age.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring Civilization Cards
walkthrough.text: Player three has been focused entirely on gathering resources and upgrading tools but has not picked up any civilization cards. Civilization cards with end-game multipliers can be worth fifteen or more points if collected early. A tool multiplier card that you hold for the whole game gains value every time you upgrade your tools.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Grab Multiplier Cards
walkthrough.text: The fix is to place a worker on a civilization card slot next round. Pay the resource cost and grab a multiplier card that matches your investments. If you have been building tools, grab a tool multiplier. If you invested in the farm, grab a farm multiplier. The earlier you get these cards, the more points they generate.

walkthrough.title: Feeding Phase
walkthrough.text: Time to feed your workers. Each player pays one food per worker. Players one through three have five workers, so they pay five food each. Player two's farm level of one produces one free food, so they only pay four from their supply. Player four also pays five food. Everyone deducts from their food supply. Make sure nobody goes hungry.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Starving on Purpose
walkthrough.text: Some new players think they can skip feeding to save food for resources. While you can choose to starve, the penalty is a devastating ten victory points. That is almost never worth it. Build your farm to avoid this entirely. By farm level five, your food is essentially free with five workers.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Build the Farm
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is straightforward. Send a worker to the Farm as often as you can in the early game. Each farm level saves one food per round for the rest of the game. A farm level of five with five workers means you never need to hunt again. That frees up workers for resource gathering and scoring opportunities.

walkthrough.title: End of Round One
walkthrough.text: Round one is complete. Workers return to your supply. Player one now has six workers. The civilization card display refills to four cards. A new building tile is revealed if one was claimed. First player rotates clockwise. You are ready for round two.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Preview
walkthrough.text: In round two, you have more workers and the board is fresh. Competition for key locations like the Mating Hut, Farm, and Tool Maker will intensify because only one player can use each per round. Prioritize the upgrade you need most and be prepared to adapt if an opponent takes your target first.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have played through your first round of Stone Age. You have seen worker placement, resource gathering with dice, permanent upgrades, and the feeding phase. Remember: invest in workers, farming, and tools early. Grab civilization cards with multipliers. And never starve on purpose unless you have no other option. You are ready to continue playing.

walkthrough.title: What to Explore Next
walkthrough.text:
Before your next game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on balancing upgrades and scoring.

Second, reference the Appendix for quick definitions of all game terms, resource costs, and scoring formulas.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Grow Your Workforce First
walkthrough.text: The Mating Hut should be one of your first stops every round until you reach ten workers. Each additional worker gives you more dice at resource locations, more placement flexibility, and more total output per round. The earlier you grow, the more rounds you benefit from the extra worker. Getting to eight or nine workers by mid-game is a hallmark of strong play.

walkthrough.title: Farm Before You Starve
walkthrough.text: Building your farm level is a compounding investment. Each level provides one free food every round for the rest of the game. A farm level of five means five free food per round, which covers five workers with zero hunting required. Start building your farm in the first two rounds and keep pushing it up whenever the Farm location is available.

walkthrough.title: Tools Smooth Out Dice Luck
walkthrough.text: Stone Age uses dice for resource gathering, which introduces randomness. Tools are the primary way to mitigate bad rolls. A tool value of six added to a poor dice roll can mean the difference between zero resources and two. Invest in tools early and use them on your most important gathering rolls, typically stone or gold where the divisor is highest.

walkthrough.title: Civilization Card Multipliers Are King
walkthrough.text: End-game scoring from civilization card multipliers can easily make up half your total score. Tool multiplier cards multiply your tool level by the number of such cards you own. Farm multipliers do the same with farm level. Building multipliers multiply by your building count. Worker multipliers multiply by your worker count. Grab these cards early so your investments have time to grow.

walkthrough.title: Unique Set Scoring
walkthrough.text: Civilization cards have different background patterns. Collecting unique patterns scores points equal to the number of unique patterns squared: one is one, two is four, three is nine, four is sixteen, five is twenty-five, six is thirty-six. Even two or three unique cards are worth a solid chunk of points. This scoring path rewards diversification alongside your main multiplier strategy.

walkthrough.title: Block Key Locations
walkthrough.text: The Farm, Tool Maker, and Mating Hut each allow only one player per round. If an opponent is racing to ten workers, placing your worker at the Mating Hut before them denies a critical upgrade. Similarly, grabbing the Farm can prevent an opponent from building food independence. Watch what your opponents need and block strategically when the timing is right.

walkthrough.title: Resource Efficiency by Type
walkthrough.text: Different resources have different dice costs. Food is the cheapest at two per die, which means the Hunting Grounds are very efficient. Gold is the most expensive at six per die, making it unreliable without tools and many workers. Focus on wood and clay early when your dice pool and tools are still small. Save gold runs for later when you have the workforce and tools to make them worthwhile.

walkthrough.title: Balance Scoring and Infrastructure
walkthrough.text: The biggest mistake in Stone Age is doing too much of one thing. A player who only builds infrastructure but never buys buildings or cards will have a great engine with nothing to show for it. Conversely, a player who scores early but never upgrades will run out of gas. The sweet spot is investing in infrastructure for the first three or four rounds, then shifting toward scoring while maintaining your farm and tools.

summary.title: Grow Your Workforce First
bullets: Visit Mating Hut until you reach 8-10 workers
More workers = more dice, placements, and output per round
Compound benefit: earlier = more rounds of extra worker

summary.title: Farm Before You Starve

Farm level 5 covers 5 workers at zero cost
Start building farm in rounds 1-2

summary.title: Tools Smooth Out Dice Luck

Most impactful on stone (÷5) and gold (÷6)
Invest early; use on your most important roll each round

summary.title: Civilization Card Multipliers Are King

Tool × cards, Farm × cards, Buildings × cards, Workers × cards
Grab early so investments have time to grow

Even 2-3 unique cards are worth solid points
Reward for diversifying alongside your main multiplier

summary.title: Block Key Locations

summary.title: Balance Scoring and Infrastructure
bullets: Infrastructure only → great engine, no points
Scoring only → runs out of gas quickly
Sweet spot: invest rounds 1-4, then shift to scoring

walkthrough.title: Worker Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: The worker rush focuses on reaching ten workers as fast as possible. Visit the Mating Hut every round until you max out. Ten workers gives you an overwhelming advantage in dice rolls and placement options. The downside is needing to feed ten mouths, so pair this with aggressive farm building. Once you hit ten workers, the sheer number of resources you gather per round will outpace everyone else.

walkthrough.title: Tool-Heavy Strategy
walkthrough.text: A tool-focused approach prioritizes the Tool Maker every round to maximize all three tool slots as quickly as possible. With twelve total tool value, your dice rolls become highly predictable. Even a mediocre roll at the gold location yields reliable returns. Pair tool investment with tool multiplier civilization cards for massive end-game scoring. Three tool multiplier cards with tool value twelve scores thirty-six bonus points.

walkthrough.title: Civilization Card Collector
walkthrough.text: This strategy prioritizes civilization cards over buildings. Multiplier cards compound with your infrastructure investments, and unique background sets provide n-squared scoring. A player with six unique backgrounds scores thirty-six points from sets alone. Combine this with four tool multiplier cards at tool level twelve for an additional forty-eight points. Card collectors often have the highest end-game scoring explosions.

walkthrough.title: Building Sprinter
walkthrough.text: Buildings score victory points immediately, which means you accumulate VP throughout the game rather than relying on a big end-game calculation. This strategy focuses on gathering resources efficiently and spending them on buildings every round. Pair it with building multiplier civilization cards for end-game bonus points on top of your in-game VP. The risk is that buildings do not improve your engine, so you need enough infrastructure first.

walkthrough.title: Farm-First Economy
walkthrough.text: Once feeding is free, every single worker can gather resources, buy cards, or build buildings. This strategy sacrifices early scoring for total long-term efficiency. The compounding food savings over eight to ten rounds more than makes up for the slow start.

walkthrough.title: Gold-Heavy Late Game
walkthrough.text: Gold is the most valuable resource but the hardest to gather. Each gold requires rolling six on a single die, which is unreliable early. But with seven workers at the gold location and tool value of nine or more, your expected yield becomes three to four gold per round. Gold is required for the most expensive buildings and civilization cards, and leftover gold scores one VP each. Save gold runs for the mid-to-late game when your infrastructure supports it.

walkthrough.title: Denial and Blocking
walkthrough.text: In a four-player game, the single-use locations create natural tension. An advanced player watches opponents' needs and blocks critical locations at the right moment. If an opponent is about to reach ten workers, grab the Mating Hut first. If someone needs the Farm to avoid starvation, take it. The goal is not to waste your own workers but to find moments where blocking hurts your opponent more than it costs you.

walkthrough.title: Reading the End-Game
walkthrough.text: Stop visiting the Mating Hut and Tool Maker. Spend all resources on buildings and the most valuable remaining civilization cards. Calculate your expected end-game multiplier scores and determine whether one more tool upgrade or one more building nets you more points. The best players know exactly when to pivot from building to scoring.

summary.title: Worker Rush Strategy
bullets: Mating Hut every round → reach 10 workers ASAP
10 workers = overwhelming dice and placement advantage
Pair with aggressive farm building to feed them all

summary.title: Tool-Heavy Strategy
bullets: Max all 3 tool slots → 12 total tool value
Makes all dice rolls predictable and efficient
3 tool multiplier cards × 12 tools = 36 bonus VP

summary.title: Civilization Card Collector
bullets: Prioritize civ cards over buildings
6 unique backgrounds = 36 VP from sets alone
Combine with multiplier cards for massive end-game scoring

summary.title: Building Sprinter
bullets: Buildings score VP immediately during the game
Pair with building multiplier civ cards
Risk: buildings don't improve your engine

summary.title: Farm-First Economy
bullets: Rush farm to level 5-7 in first few rounds
Free feeding → all workers go to resources/scoring
Slow start but massive long-term efficiency

summary.title: Gold-Heavy Late Game
bullets: Gold: ÷6 per die, unreliable early
7 workers + 9+ tools → 3-4 gold per round
Save gold runs for mid-to-late game

summary.title: Denial and Blocking
bullets: Single-use locations create blocking opportunities
Block when it hurts opponent more than it costs you
Watch: Mating Hut, Farm, Tool Maker

summary.title: Reading the End-Game
bullets: Shift from infrastructure to pure scoring near game end
Stop upgrades → spend all resources on buildings/cards
Calculate: 1 more tool upgrade vs 1 more building?

entries.definition: Meeples placed on board locations each round. Start with five, expandable to ten via the Mating Hut. More workers means more dice rolls and placement options.
Tokens consumed during the feeding phase. Each worker requires one food per round. Farm level reduces food needed. Remaining food is worth one VP at end of game.
Resource gathered from the Forest location. Dice total divided by three. Used to pay for buildings and civilization cards.
Resource gathered from the Clay Pit location. Dice total divided by four. Also called brick. Used for buildings and civilization cards.
Resource gathered from the Quarry location. Dice total divided by five. More expensive to gather but used for high-value buildings.
The most valuable resource, gathered from the River location. Dice total divided by six. Unreliable without tools and many workers. Worth one VP at end of game.
Board location for gathering food. Unlimited worker capacity. Roll dice and divide total by two for food received.
Board location for gathering wood. Maximum seven total workers. Roll dice and divide by three.
Board location for gathering clay. Maximum seven total workers. Roll dice and divide by four.
Board location for gathering stone. Maximum seven total workers. Roll dice and divide by five.
Board location for gathering gold. Maximum seven total workers. Roll dice and divide by six.
Board location that increases farm level by one per visit. Exactly one worker from one player per round. Maximum farm level ten.
Board location that increases tool level by one per visit. Exactly one worker from one player per round. Maximum total tool value twelve.
Board location that grants one new worker. Requires exactly two workers from one player per round. Maximum ten workers.
Cards purchased from the display by placing one worker and paying resources. Provide immediate bonuses and end-game multiplier scoring. Thirty-six cards total.
Tiles purchased by placing one worker and paying the shown resource combination. Score VP immediately. Top tile of each stack is visible.
A permanent bonus that adds to dice totals when gathering resources. Three tool slots per player, each upgradeable from one to four. Each tool used once per round.
Tracks how much free food you produce each round during feeding. Each level provides one food automatically. Maximum level ten.
End-of-round phase where each player must pay one food per worker. Farm level provides free food. Failure to feed causes a ten VP penalty.
If you cannot feed all workers, you lose ten victory points. You may choose to starve deliberately, but the penalty is steep. You never lose workers.
A civilization card end-game scoring type. VP equals tool level multiplied by number of tool multiplier cards owned.
A civilization card end-game scoring type. VP equals farm level multiplied by number of farm multiplier cards owned.
A civilization card end-game scoring type. VP equals number of buildings purchased multiplied by number of building multiplier cards owned.
A civilization card end-game scoring type. Also called shaman multiplier. VP equals number of workers multiplied by number of worker multiplier cards owned.

Civilization cards with different background patterns score VP equal to the number of unique patterns squared. One is one, two is four, three is nine, four is sixteen, five is twenty-five, six is thirty-six.
Accumulated from buildings, card bonuses, and end-game multipliers. Remaining resources and food each worth one VP. Most VP wins.
The divisor for each resource type when rolling dice: Food divides by two, Wood by three, Clay by four, Stone by five, Gold by six.
Each die averages three point five. With three workers at Stone (divisor five), expected yield is three point five times three divided by five, which equals two point one, rounded down to two stone.
Individual board tracking farm level, tool slots, and resource storage. Public information that all players can see.
Track on the main board where score markers move. Tracks in-game VP from buildings and card bonuses. End-game multipliers added at final scoring.
